top_categories.workout_context: 502 miles. Your legs will beg to quit after 120. This trains the power output you need from mile 130 onward.
63,170ft of climbing means repeated surges above threshold. VO2max work is how you survive the fifth climb, not just the first.
Every hard session works better with a bigger aerobic base. At 502 miles, base fitness is the difference between racing and surviving.
Race-pace efforts that mimic Haute Route Alps' demands. Pacing, fueling, and tactical decisions under fatigue. Practice the race before August.
At Haute Route Alps, surges push you above threshold and you have to recover while still riding. Over-unders train exactly that.
502 miles rewards fat oxidation. This volume work shifts your fuel mix so you're still burning fat at hour 5, not bonking at hour 3.
63,170ft of climbing on grades that change every minute. You need to switch from seated to standing without losing rhythm.
126ft/mi means long efforts at or near FTP on every climb. If your threshold cracks at minute 15, you'll walk the rest.
Haute Route Alps' Paved Alpine mountain roads, Tour de France and Giro forces constant power changes. Surge over the rough stuff, settle on the smooth, repeat for 502 miles.
Endurance with intensity spikes baked in. Exactly what 63,170ft of climbing on paved Alpine mountain passes does to your power file on race day.
88–92% FTP is where you'll spend the hardest sustained miles of Haute Route Alps. Train it until it feels boring.
Low-cadence force work for 63,170ft of climbing. The slow, grinding ascents where you can't spin—you push.

race_overlay.heat: August in Nice to Thonon-les-Bains, French Alps can bring heat. Complete 3–4 heat exposure sessions in the final 2 weeks before Haute Route Alps. Increase sodium intake 48 hours before race day.
race_overlay.nutrition: Ultra-distance fueling for 502 miles: 80–100g carbs/hour from mile 1 — don’t wait until you’re hungry. Practice your exact race-day nutrition in every long training ride. Carry backup calories. 502 miles burns 8,000–12,000+ calories — you cannot replace them all, but you must try.
race_overlay.altitude: Haute Route Alps with 63,170ft of climbing, much of it above 8,000ft demands altitude preparation. Arrive 5–7 days early for acclimatization. Expect 5–15% power reduction at altitude. Increase iron intake 4 weeks out. Hydrate aggressively — altitude increases fluid loss by 20–40%. Summit temperatures below 5C at 2,700m+ with wind chill well below zero; Rapid weather changes at altitude: clear to whiteout in 20 minutes on exposed cols.

pack_summary: This 10-workout pack focuses on Durability, VO2max, and Endurance to prepare you for 502 miles of paved alpine mountain passes in Nice to Thonon-les-Bains, French Alps.
